segments.title: Close, loose, phantom, and distant blockades
segments.summary: A compact taxonomy separates close, loose, phantom, and distant blockades by distance, deception, and strategic purpose.
segments.body: This section turns blockade from a single label into several operational choices. Close blockades sit near enemy ports, loose and phantom blockades exploit uncertainty over the main fleet's position, and distant blockades use strategic chokepoints to attack trade rather than one harbor. It is a useful entry point for understanding why blockade design depends on the target, the available ships, and the commander's appetite for risk.

evidence.note: The transcript defines close blockade and contrasts naval ports with merchant ports.
The lecture explains loose and phantom blockade signaling around a fleet over the horizon.
The distant blockade discussion shifts to strategic points such as Gibraltar, Singapore, and canals.

segments.title: Blockade as exercised sea control
segments.summary: The lecture frames blockade as practical sea control and explains why surface ships, not submarines alone, usually carry the policing burden.
segments.body: The watch point links blockade to command of the sea rather than treating it as a legal declaration. Dr. Clarke pushes back on the idea that submarines are the natural tool for this role, because stopping, inspecting, and controlling trade generally requires surface ships with boarding capacity. That distinction matters for modern force-structure debates as much as for sail-era blockades.

evidence.note: The transcript states that blockading is about exercising control of the sea.
The submarine comparison ends with the requirement for surface ships.
Older cruisers and boarding/search crews are identified as practical blockade assets.

segments.title: Attempts to blockade Britain
segments.summary: German submarine campaigns and Napoleon's Continental System are compared as different efforts to pressure Britain through maritime trade.
segments.body: This segment shows why a blockade can fail when the enforcing power cannot actually control movement at sea. The First and Second World War submarine campaigns are presented as attempts to use undersea warfare against British trade, while Napoleon's legal blockade is treated as much weaker because merchant ships could still sail toward the best price. The contrast helps explain the difference between proclamation, interdiction, and genuine command of maritime routes.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses German submarine operations as blockade attempts against Britain.
Napoleon's method is described as forbidding European trade with Britain by law.
The lecture notes British Baltic influence and the French problem of being blockaded themselves.

segments.title: Blockade runners, coppering, and prize incentives
segments.summary: Blockade running becomes a story of ship speed, neutral flags, prize money, and the diplomatic risks of boarding merchantmen.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke connects coppered hulls to blockade work: reducing fouling kept ships fast enough to catch valuable runners. He then separates runners into shore-hugging small craft, shallow-draft fast ships, and vessels trying to pass as neutral. The result is a practical view of blockade enforcement where prize incentives, neutral rights, boarding etiquette, and naval performance all interact.

evidence.note: The transcript links coppering to speed, catching blockade runners, and prize value.
Three forms of blockade runner are described, including small coastal craft, Dutch-style shallow-draft ships, and false neutrals.
The lecture explains the danger of neutral claims and the need for careful boarding behavior.

segments.title: Sustaining a blockade at sea
segments.summary: The logistics chapter explains how food, livestock, vegetables, refit cycles, and relief ships kept a blockading fleet on station.
segments.body: This watch point makes blockade look less like a line on a map and more like a sustainment system. The lecture argues that replenishment at sea predates the world wars and describes small merchant vessels moving meat, livestock, and vegetables out to fleets. It also notes that every visible blockading ship implies more ships behind it for relief, refit, and convoyed supplies.

evidence.note: The transcript rejects the idea that replenishment at sea began in the world wars.
Small vessels are described carrying food, livestock, and fresh vegetables to the fleet.
The lecture explains relief, refit, supply ships, and the hidden force needed behind the visible blockade.

segments.title: Toulon's inshore squadron and third-rate risk
segments.summary: Toulon's blockade shows why the Royal Navy sometimes risked third-rate ships close inshore when the enemy fleet was dangerous enough.
segments.body: The Toulon example explains the force mix behind a close watch on a major naval base. Four third rates could form an inshore squadron when the fleet inside was powerful, even though large ships near the coast faced greater navigational and weather risk. The discussion then ties that choice back to convoy escorts and Gibraltar-based sustainment, because replacing and supplying those ships required a larger support system.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Pellew's Toulon inshore squadron and the use of third rates.
The named ships and risk of using valuable third rates close to shore are discussed.
The supply convoy and Gibraltar support logic are tied to the inshore squadron.

segments.title: The 5 November 1813 Toulon action
segments.summary: The Toulon action illustrates how a small blockade clash could reinforce French caution even without producing a decisive fleet battle.
segments.body: This final case study walks through the French attempt to exercise from Toulon, the British inshore squadron's response, and the rapid arrival of heavier British ships. Dr. Clarke frames the action against the War of 1812 to show why Britain's major fleet strength stayed concentrated against France rather than North America. The outcome was not a crushing battle, but it strengthened the psychological effect of the blockade by making the French believe the main fleet was always close.

evidence.note: The opposing French and British forces at Toulon are listed and contrasted.
The lecture explains the French exercise, wind shift, and Heathcote's decision to attack.
The inshore squadron, Pompeii, Caledonia, San Josef, and Boyne join the action as the French return toward Toulon.
The conclusion explains how the clash made the French overestimate the tightness of the blockade.
